frozen_components: residual stream activations (frozen during probe training)
architecture.summary: CORAL is an inference-time steering method that trains weight-decay regularized MLP probes on frozen residual stream activations to predict residual correctness (the gap between ideal and actual probability distributions). At inference, the probe's predictions are centered and additively applied to shift base probabilities toward better calibration and accuracy, without modifying model weights.

biblio.title: Correctness-Optimized Residual Activation Lens (CORAL): Transferrable and Calibration-Aware Inference-Time Steering

claims.statement: CORAL consistently improves accuracy by 10% and expected calibration error (ECE) by 50% on average across three 7B-parameter models

CORAL demonstrates transfer to the complete published test sets of four held-out benchmarks (ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, Math-MC, OpenBookQA), averaging 14% accuracy improvements and 49% ECE improvements
On RACE with Deepseek-LLM-7B-Chat, CORAL outperforms the second-best method by 17.39 percentage points in accuracy and achieves 64% relative improvement in ECE
Probe training requires under 5 hours on a single GPU with 8.4k–10k training questions
Middle layers (17–21) of DeepSeek-7B-Chat contain the most useful correctness signals for steering, with layers 17–21 yielding the strongest improvements in both accuracy and calibration

Individual SAE features produce negligible causal effects on model calibration, with mean ECE change from single-neuron ablation equal to 0.0125 percentage points, and aggregating top features by ablation impact does not improve accuracy or calibration
Multi-layer CORAL, where probes are trained on concatenated activations from the top 3–5 layers, produced no meaningful improvement over single-layer steering
CORAL's evaluation is limited to multiple-choice question answering, leaving open whether correctness signals generalize to free-form generation where correctness is harder to define
